2004 - KIWI #669|publisher=nzleague.co.nz g|date=2 April 2012|accessdate=1 January 2013}} |image = Logan Swann.jpg |image_size = |position = {{rlp|SR|LK}} |birth_date = {{birth date and age|df=yes|1975|02|10}} |birth_place = [[Auckland, New Zealand]] |height = {{convert|193|cm|ftin|abbr=on}} |weight = {{convert|104|kg|stlb|abbr=on}} |club1 = {{nowrap|[[New Zealand Warriors]]}} |year1start = 1997 |year1end = 03 |appearances1 = 146 |tries1 = 34 |goals1 = 1 |fieldgoals1 = 0 |points1 = 138 |club2 = [[Bradford Bulls]] |year2start = 2004 |year2end = |appearances2 = 26 |tries2 = 6 |goals2 = 0 |fieldgoals2 = 0 |points2 = 24 |club3 = [[Warrington Wolves]] |year3start = 2005 |year3end = 06 |appearances3 = 52 |tries3 = 20 |goals3 = 0 |fieldgoals3 = 0 |points3 = 80 |club4 = [[New Zealand Warriors]] |year4start = 2007 |year4end = 08 |appearances4 = 49 |tries4 = 2 |goals4 = 0 |fieldgoals4 = 0 |points4 = 8 |teamA = [[New Zealand national rugby league team|New Zealand]] |yearAstart = 1996 |yearAend = 04 |appearancesA = 29 |triesA = 5 |goalsA = 0 |fieldgoalsA = 0 |pointsA = 20 |teamB = [[New Zealand national rugby league team|All Golds]] |yearBstart = 2008 |yearBend = |appearancesB = 1 |triesB = 0 |goalsB = 1 |fieldgoalsB = 0 |pointsB = 2 |retired = yes |source = {{cite web|url=https://www.rugbyleagueproject.org/players/logan-swann/summary.html|title=Statistics at rugbyleagueproject.org|publisher=rugbyleagueproject.org|date=31 December 2017|accessdate=1 January 2018}} |updated = 26 May 2010 | module = {{infobox person | embed = yes | father = | mother = | spouse = | education = | alma_mater = | relatives = [[Anthony Swann]] (cousin)
[[Willie Swann]] (cousin) }} }} '''Logan Swann''' (born 10 February 1975) is a New Zealand former professional [[rugby league]] footballer who represented [[New Zealand national rugby league team|New Zealand]]. Swann is related to several other international rugby league players, notably cousins [[Willie Swann]] and [[Anthony Swann]]. ==Early years== Swann was born in [[Auckland]], New Zealand, on 10 February 1975. He was educated at [[Sacred Heart College, Auckland]]. He started his junior career in New Zealand with the [[Mt Wellington Warriors|Mt Wellington]] club, then moved to the [[Ōtāhuhu]] club, completing his amateur league years with the [[Ellerslie Eagles]].{{fact|date=May 2022}} ==Playing career== ===National Rugby League=== Swann joined the [[Auckland Warriors]] Colts side in 1995 and later went on to play in the Reserves team in 1996. At the end of the 1996 season Swann had impressed the Reserve grade coach [[Frank Endacott]] so much that he was picked for the [[New Zealand national rugby league team]] squad. Swann was picked for the [[New Zealand national rugby league team]] in 1996 straight from the Reserve Grade of the Auckland Warriors. His test début was against the Great Britain team. Swann went on to play for [[New Zealand national rugby league team|New Zealand]] twenty nine times over nine years from 1996–2004. Swann made his first grade début in 1997. In 1998 he represented [[Samoa national rugby league team|Samoa]] in the [[Super League World Nines]], and played in the final. Swann was selected for the [[New Zealand national rugby league team|New Zealand team]] to compete in the end of season [[1999 Rugby League Tri-Nations]] tournament. In the final against [[Australia national rugby league team|Australia]] he played as a {{rlp|lf}} in the Kiwis' 22–20 loss. Swann played for New Zealand in the [[2000 Rugby League World Cup]]. He stayed with the Warriors for seven seasons, winning the Minor Premiership in 2002. He played from the interchange bench in the [[2002 NRL grand final]] which the Warriors lost to the [[Sydney Roosters]]. In May 2003, Swann and two associates assaulted a man on [[Queen Street, Auckland]]. The victim was left with a swollen eye and facial grazes.{{Cite news |date=26 November 2004 |title=Warrior guilty of assault |work=[[New Zealand Herald]] |url=https://www.nzherald.co.nz/nz/crime/warrior-guilty-of-assault/K3BAJA7E3YO4JLDXF25MDTB6CU/ |access-date=29 November 2022}} In December 2004, Swann appeared before Judge [[Avinash Deobhakta]] for sentencing after earlier pleading guilty to common assault. Saying that he was "very, very reluctantly" granting him a discharge without conviction, Judge Deobhakta ordered Swann to pay the victim $500. "People have the wrong impression that guys like you with celebrity status can get away with bad behaviour under the influence of high intoxicants, just because you are sporting celebrities," he said, before noting that it was "glowing testimonies", a desire not to ruin Swann's career and no previous convictions that had tipped the balance in Swann's favour.{{Cite web |title=Judge Avinash Ganesh Deobhakta, 1936 - 2015 |url=https://www.lawsociety.org.nz/news/legal-news/obituaries/obituaries-list/judge-avinash-ganesh-deobhakta-1936-2015/ |access-date=29 November 2022 |website=New Zealand Law Society}} ===Super League=== In 2004 Swann moved to England, joining the [[Bradford Bulls]].{{cite web|url=https://www.encyclopedia.com/doc/1G1-109158535.html|title=Logan Swann at encyclopedia.com|publisher=Liverpool Daily Post|date=22 October 2003|accessdate=1 January 2004}}{{cite web|url=https://www.independent.co.uk/sport/general/rugby-league/bradford-lure-swann-and-set-sights-on-kohelove-584191.html|title=Bradford lure Swann and set sights on Kohe-Love|publisher=independent.co.uk|date=22 October 2003|accessdate=1 January 2004}} Having won [[Super League VIII]], Bradford played against 2003 NRL Premiers, the [[Penrith Panthers]] in the [[2004 World Club Challenge]]. Swann played as a {{rlp|lf}} and scored a [[Try (rugby)|try]] in the Bulls' 22–4 victory. He played for Bradford at {{rlp|sr}} in their [[2004 Super League Grand Final]] loss against the [[Leeds Rhinos]]. In 2005 he joined the [[Warrington Wolves]] where he spent two seasons. ===Return to the NRL=== Swann returned home to play for the [[New Zealand Warriors]] in the 2007 and 2008 seasons.{{cite news|url=http://www.nzherald.co.nz/sport/news/article.cfm?c_id=4&objectid=10404857|title=League: Logan returns for Warriors Swann-song|author=Brown, Michael|date=8 October 2006|work=[[The New Zealand Herald]]|accessdate=15 October 2011}} This means that Swann has been involved in five of the seven playoff campaigns for the [[New Zealand Warriors]] up to end of the 2008 year; 2001, 2002, 2003, 2007 and 2008. On 8 April 2007 Swann became the third player to play 150 first grade games for the Warriors.{{cite web|url=http://rleague.com/db/article.php?id=27727|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20150208203843/http://rleague.com/db/article.php?id=27727|url-status=dead|archive-date=8 February 2015|title=Swann Eyes 150th Appearances for Warriors|publisher=rleague.com|date=3 April 2007|accessdate=1 January 2008}} In 2008 he played for the [[New Zealand national rugby league team|All Golds]] in their match against the [[New Zealand Māori rugby league team|New Zealand Māori]] that served as a buildup game to the [[2008 Rugby League World Cup|2008 World Cup]].John Lazo-Ron [http://www.times.co.nz/cms/sport/sports_news/2008/10/swann_grateful_to_have_played_alongside_legend_ruben_wiki.php Swann grateful to have played alongside legend Ruben Wiki] {{webarchive|url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110727183936/http://www.times.co.nz/cms/sport/sports_news/2008/10/swann_grateful_to_have_played_alongside_legend_ruben_wiki.php |date=2011-07-27}} ''Howick and Pakuranga Times'', 9 October 2008 ==Post playing== After retiring at the end of 2008 Swann became a [[real estate]] salesperson.{{cite news|url=http://www.stuff.co.nz/sunday-news/sport/669010|title=Logan's run at an end|date=11 October 2008|work=[[Sunday News (New Zealand)|Sunday News]]|accessdate=15 October 2011}} He currently works for [[Ray White]] New Zealand in the suburb of Remuera.{{cite web|url=http://msn.realestate.co.nz/offices/22063/agents/29075|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20110822161007/http://msn.realestate.co.nz/offices/22063/agents/29075|url-status=dead|archive-date=22 August 2011|title=Realestate Profile for Logan Swann|publisher=msn.realestate.co.nz|date=31 December 2017|accessdate=1 January 2018}} In November 2020 he was named one of the best dressed men on [[David Hartnell|David Hartnell MNZM's]] Best Dressed List.{{cite web|title=The best dressed Kiwis list is out, who makes the cut?|url=https://www.stuff.co.nz/entertainment/300162841/the-best-dressed-kiwis-list-is-out-who-makes-the-cut|work=[[Stuff (website)|Stuff]] |date=22 November 2020|access-date=22 November 2020}}
